narrative_blocks.header: A valedictorian who wrote for Britannica
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Gleason Newton graduated valedictorian of Yale Law’s class of 1872, taking prizes for the best common-law and civil-law essays. He went on to author the Encyclopedia Britannica’s article on bankruptcy and the probate-law chapter of Townsend’s edition of the Civil Officer.

narrative_blocks.header: He had himself unseated from office
narrative_blocks.body: Re-elected to the General Assembly in 1886 by a single vote, Newton discovered the count was in error — and, acting as attorney for his own opponent, contested the result and had himself unseated on the second day of the session, the shortest tenure on record.

narrative_blocks.header: He argued through insomnia and a speech defect
narrative_blocks.body: Like his ancestor Burwell Newton, Henry struggled to make himself understood, and suffered lifelong insomnia from boyhood. An indomitable persistence carried him over both, and he became one of the foremost lawyers in Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Counsel to a bank and a presidential candidate
narrative_blocks.body: His clients included the Yale National Bank, Brown Brothers of New York, and William Jennings Bryan in the matter of the Bennett will. He argued many causes before the courts of last resort and was much consulted by the public and the bar alike.

notable.notable_blurb: Yale Law valedictorian; Connecticut legislator and jurist

career.organization: U.S. District Court (appt. Judge William K. Townsend)

education.degree: A.B. 1870; Phi Beta Kappa, Eclectic Fraternity
LL.B. 1872, valedictorian; prizes for best common-law and civil-law essays
